title: UCB — Immunology — Thesis
html:
Bimzelx Forward-Looking Thesis -- 5 Strategic Bets

Thesis 1: Bimzelx Will Carve a Durable "Speed + Joints" Niche Against IL-23 Dominance, But Will Not Overtake Skyrizi in Overall PsO Share

The Bet: Bimzelx will establish itself as the preferred biologic for patients who need rapid onset of action and/or have concurrent joint involvement (PsO+PsA), but Skyrizi will retain market leadership in overall plaque psoriasis prescribing through 2028.

Evidence For:

  • BE BOLD head-to-head data (March 2026) demonstrates statistically significant superiority of bimekizumab over risankizumab on ACR50 at week 16 in PsA -- this is the first licensed biologic to beat an IL-23 inhibitor in PsA. This is a powerful differentiating data point.
  • ZoomRx HCP-Pt data shows HCPs already frame Bimzelx as "fast acting" with "the best data for both skin and joints." In conversations where joint symptoms are present, Bimzelx and Taltz are preferentially positioned.
  • IL-17s act faster than IL-23s (clinically visible improvement in 2-4 weeks vs. 8-12 weeks for IL-23s). This matters for patients with high symptom burden and QoL urgency -- which describes over 50% of the ZoomRx sample.
  • Scalp psoriasis (discussed in 57% of conversations) responds well to IL-17s, and HCPs cite scalp efficacy as a driver for Bimzelx.

Evidence Against:

  • Skyrizi holds ~45% of US biologic prescription share in psoriasis with $17.3B in expected 2025 sales. This installed base creates massive prescribing inertia.
  • IL-23 class benefits from longer drug survival in real-world data, meaning fewer switches and more durable relationships.
  • Skyrizi's quarterly dosing (every 12 weeks maintenance) is a powerful patient convenience advantage that Bimzelx cannot match (every 4-8 weeks).
  • Familiarity bias is deeply entrenched: ZoomRx data shows 23 of 30 HCPs present only a single product, and most default to what they know. Displacing Skyrizi as the default requires sustained commercial investment.

Implication for ZoomRx: The "speed + joints" positioning creates a definable patient archetype that ZoomRx research can quantify. Future studies should size the "rapid-onset-seeking + joint-involved" segment and track whether BE BOLD data changes prescribing behavior among Bimzelx Dabblers and Convert IL-17A Writers.

Thesis 2: Hidradenitis Suppurativa Will Be Bimzelx's Highest-Growth Indication Through 2027, But Realization Depends on Access Infrastructure

The Bet: The HS indication (approved November 2024) will contribute a disproportionate share of Bimzelx's incremental revenue growth through 2027, potentially becoming the second-largest revenue contributor after PsO by 2028.

Evidence For:

  • HS is severely underserved: prior to 2024, Humira was the only approved biologic, and Humira faces massive biosimilar erosion. The competitive vacuum is real.
  • HS patient population is substantial (~1% of the US population, many undiagnosed or undertreated), and disease burden is extremely high (pain, scarring, social isolation).
  • Bimzelx is the first IL-17A/F inhibitor approved for HS. The dual mechanism targeting is clinically differentiated from adalimumab (TNF), which has modest HS efficacy.
  • BE HEARD I and II trials (1,014 patients) showed sustained response from week 16 through week 48 -- durable efficacy that supports long-term treatment.
  • UCB's FY 2025 earnings commentary explicitly highlighted HS momentum as a growth driver.

Evidence Against:

  • HS prescribers (dermatologists, surgeons) may have less familiarity with Bimzelx compared to psoriasis prescribers. The familiarity barrier that ZoomRx data revealed in PsO will be even more pronounced in HS.
  • Insurance access for biologics in HS is historically more restrictive than in PsO. Step-through requirements may mandate adalimumab failure first.
  • Cosentyx (secukinumab) is also being evaluated in HS, and if approved, would bring Novartis's established access network into competition.
  • Patient identification and diagnosis in HS remain challenging -- average diagnostic delay is 7-10 years, limiting the speed at which any therapy can penetrate.

Implication for ZoomRx: HS represents a greenfield research opportunity. UCB will need HCP-Pt conversation data, patient journey mapping, and promotional effectiveness tracking in HS specifically. ZoomRx should proactively propose HS-specific research modules.

Thesis 3: The Safety Perception Gap -- Not Actual Safety -- Is Bimzelx's Biggest Commercial Obstacle, and It Is Addressable

The Bet: Bimzelx's safety concerns (candidiasis, suicidal ideation screening, GI/IBD) are perception-driven barriers more than clinical realities, and targeted messaging can close the gap within 12-18 months of intervention.

Evidence For:

  • ZoomRx conversation data reveals a striking disconnect: most HCPs "aren't actually concerned with the risk of suicidal thoughts with Bimzelx, but they often don't want to put in the effort to explain it to their patients, considering time constraints." This is an effort-aversion problem, not a safety problem.
  • Safety concerns were cited as barriers even by 4 of 7 Bimzelx Believers -- the segment most familiar with the drug. This suggests the issue is less about knowledge and more about conversational friction.
  • Candidiasis is consistently described by prescribing HCPs as "very treatable" and "once it's treated the first time it doesn't come back." The actual clinical management is straightforward, but the label warning creates disproportionate apprehension.
  • GI/IBD screening is the only safety concern that demonstrably changes prescribing (one patient diverted from Bimzelx to Skyrizi due to GI symptoms). This is a legitimate clinical signal but affects a subset of patients, not the broad population.

Evidence Against:

  • The depression/suicidal ideation label warning, even if clinically rare, creates legal and medicolegal discomfort for HCPs. This cannot be messaged away -- it requires institutional reassurance.
  • Safety concerns are amplified by competitive detailing. AbbVie and Lilly sales forces may highlight Bimzelx's safety profile as a competitive vulnerability.
  • Real-world pharmacovigilance data is still accumulating. If a high-profile adverse event occurs, it could reinforce the safety narrative disproportionately.

Implication for ZoomRx: This thesis directly informs HCP-Pt conversation research design. ZoomRx should propose a follow-up study specifically examining how HCPs discuss Bimzelx safety when provided with new messaging frameworks (e.g., simplified scripts for suicidal ideation disclosure, candidiasis management reassurance). Measuring changes in safety discussion patterns over time is a valuable longitudinal metric.

Thesis 4: The "Single Product Presentation" Behavior Is Both Bimzelx's Greatest Threat and Opportunity

The Bet: The finding that 23 of 30 HCPs present only one product to patients after route of administration is decided means that commercial success depends almost entirely on becoming the default product in the HCP's mind -- not on patient-facing messaging.

Evidence For:

  • ZoomRx data shows once route of administration (oral vs. injectable) is decided (largely patient-driven), the product choice is HCP-driven and typically singular. HCPs are "aware that patients can feel overloaded with treatment options, leading them to narrow down choices implicitly."
  • Familiarity bias is the dominant behavioral driver: "HCPs have limited time with patients and want to get buy-in as soon as possible while minimizing discordance."
  • Of 12 HCPs who had UCB rep interactions in the past month, only 4 prescribed Bimzelx -- suggesting that single rep interactions are insufficient to shift the default product.
  • The behavioral science framework from ZoomRx identifies three reinforcing biases: effort aversion (avoid difficulty), familiarity bias (default to what's known), and framing effect (order impacts receptivity). All three favor the incumbent product.

Evidence Against:

  • The BE BOLD superiority data provides a concrete, headline-friendly reason for HCPs to reconsider their default. "First biologic to beat an IL-23 in PsA" is a compelling narrative change catalyst.
  • UCB's rapid indication expansion (5 indications in 13 months) increases the number of touchpoints and reasons for HCPs to encounter Bimzelx across different patient populations.
  • Bimzelx's 200%+ revenue growth in 2025 demonstrates that defaults are shifting, even if gradually.

Implication for ZoomRx: ZoomRx should track the "default product" metric longitudinally. In each wave of HCP-Pt conversations, measuring which product an HCP presents as their single recommendation -- and whether that shifts over time from Skyrizi/Taltz to Bimzelx -- is the single most important commercial indicator. This is more predictive than awareness or consideration metrics.

Thesis 5: Bimzelx's Multi-Indication Platform Will Drive "Halo Prescribing" Across Conditions, Making the Dermatologist-Rheumatologist Bridge a Strategic Priority

The Bet: As Bimzelx gains traction in PsA (with BE BOLD data), axSpA, and HS alongside PsO, dermatologists who develop comfort with Bimzelx in one indication will increasingly prescribe it across conditions. Similarly, positive rheumatology experience will create referral-back loops to dermatology. This cross-pollination will be a significant growth accelerator by 2027.

Evidence For:

  • ZoomRx data shows 6 of 7 PsO+PsA patients are managed solely by dermatologists, meaning a dermatologist comfortable prescribing Bimzelx for skin can readily extend to joints without rheumatology involvement.
  • UCB's 5-indication portfolio in 13 months creates a "one drug, multiple problems" narrative that simplifies HCP decision-making. Effort aversion (a documented HCP bias) favors using a single agent they already know across conditions.
  • Bimzelx's EUR 2.23B in 2025 sales (from EUR 607M in 2024) -- a >200% jump -- suggests the multi-indication flywheel is already spinning, even in early stages.
  • The HS approval broadens Bimzelx's presence in dermatology offices beyond psoriasis, creating additional prescribing comfort.

Evidence Against:

  • Multi-indication platforms have not always translated to halo prescribing (see: Humira's broad indications vs. specialist siloing in practice).
  • Skyrizi is building its own cross-indication story with Crohn's and UC approvals, and AbbVie has far more commercial firepower.
  • Rheumatologist prescribing behavior is distinct from dermatologist behavior, and UCB may lack the rheumatology sales force depth to compete with Lilly (Taltz) and Novartis (Cosentyx) in that channel.

Implication for ZoomRx: ZoomRx should propose integrated tracker studies that capture Bimzelx prescribing across conditions within the same HCP panel. Understanding whether a dermatologist who prescribes Bimzelx for PsO begins using it for HS or PsA (without separate intervention) would be a high-value insight for UCB commercial strategy. The PET tracker should include cross-indication prescribing as a standard metric.
